_comment: Human-readable changelog for the dashboard's About tab -- written for people, not commit archaeologists. The dashboard fetches this from GitHub at page load (baked copy as fallback), so add an entry here with each release and every install sees it. Newest first. Dates are YYYY-MM-DD.

entries.title: Dials and digits move as one (firmware 1.25)
notes: New firmware 1.25: the ring gauges and dial now update in lockstep with their digits. When the number rolls, the arc sweeps -- same moment, same duration, same easing -- so each readout has one coherent update beat every few seconds instead of a needle that moves ahead of the numbers.
If a value changes while the digits are in their between-rolls hold, the gauge holds with them and they fire together when the hold ends.
Tiny fluctuations that don't change the displayed number no longer nudge the arc at all -- it stays planted until there's a real update to show.
Flash from the General tab after updating. No dashboard or server changes.

entries.title: Gauges sweep instead of snapping (firmware 1.24)
notes: New firmware 1.24: the ring gauges and the dial now ease smoothly between values instead of jumping -- about half a second of gentle sweep, the same easing curve as the rolling digits. Flash from the General tab after updating.
A new value arriving mid-sweep retargets from wherever the arc currently is, so the motion never stutters or jumps backward.
The dial's color follows the eased value too, so it fades through its zones as the needle sweeps rather than flicking.

Design note: unlike the digits (which hold and roll every few seconds for readability), the gauges move freely -- an arc in smooth motion is how analog instruments behave. The needle leads, the odometer follows.
No dashboard or server changes.

entries.title: Smoother, steadier rolling numbers (firmware 1.23)
notes: New firmware 1.23, all about polish on the rolling numbers after seeing them on real hardware. Flash from the General tab after updating.

Fixed the dark 'blips' around rolling digits on layouts with a background glow (like the temperature mist page). The animation used to erase its working area with solid black; it now snapshots and restores the exact pixels it touches, so any background survives untouched.

Fixed the percent sign (and neighboring digits) shifting sideways at the start and end of a roll. Characters now sit at exactly their natural printed positions during the animation -- an unchanged character doesn't move by a single pixel -- and when a value changes width (99% to 100%), the surviving characters glide smoothly to their new homes instead of snapping.
On the dial layout, the small percent sign now rides the digits' live edge during a roll instead of waiting at the destination.
No dashboard or server changes.

entries.title: Rolling numbers, a real splash screen, and a 1.3" fit (firmware 1.22)
notes: New firmware 1.22 -- flash from the General tab after updating. The headline: numbers on the physical screen now roll odometer-style when they change, on every layout. Digits slide up when a value grows and down when it falls, with the trailing digit staying planted like a proper odometer.
Readability rule built in: a readout rolls at most once every 4 seconds. A jittery metric holds steady and then rolls calmly to the freshest value instead of flickering.

New Aspect Ratio option: 1.3" compact. It shrinks the picture to a 200-pixel centered square -- the exact physical size of a 1.3" board's glass -- so a 240x280 display mounted behind a case with a 1.3" cutout shows everything with nothing hidden at the edges.
The Temperature screensaver now uses the same typeface and degree format as the Temperature layout page, dead-center on the physical panel, instead of the old blocky scaled font.

While waiting for the first stats from the host, the screen now shows the TinyScreen wordmark with 'loading...' underneath -- a proper splash screen instead of empty metrics with a tiny 'waiting for host' note.
Older dashboards still work: the previous square-fit setting maps onto the new aspect modes automatically.

entries.title: The computer-path setup step stops lying
notes: Fixed first-time setup over a computer's USB port leaving the display unconfigured: opening the serial connection can restart these displays, and the settings used to be sent while it was still booting -- then reported as 'likely worked'. The wizard now waits for the display to finish booting, sends the settings, verifies the display confirmed them, and retries a couple of times if it didn't.
If the display truly never confirms, the wizard now says so plainly -- nothing was saved, run the step again -- instead of guessing optimistically.
On success, the wizard mentions the screen will light up within a few seconds (it restarts once to bring the display up), so you know what 'worked' looks like before unplugging it.
The 'Run first-time setup' links shown for an unconfigured display now match the app's colors instead of rendering as default blue/purple links.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: The wrong-board trap is closed (firmware 1.21)
notes: Root cause of the mystery clicking and vanishing displays, found at last: a freshly-flashed, unconfigured display looked to the dashboard like a configured 1.3" board, and saving any setting then genuinely configured it as one. The 1.3" pin layout collides with the USB wiring on other boards, knocking them off the bus the moment drawing starts -- the rhythmic clicking.

Fixed at every layer: the dashboard now shows an unconfigured display as 'New display detected' with a pointer to first-time setup (and keeps Layouts/Screen read-only until then); ordinary saves no longer mention the board at all; and firmware 1.21 refuses to consider itself configured until a command explicitly names its board. Only the setup wizard does that.

If your display is currently stuck clicking: do one more full-erase flash from the wizard, unplug and replug it WITHOUT holding the BOOT button, then complete the wizard's setup step and pick your actual board. With this release, that state can't be created again.
Settings changes sent to an unconfigured display still apply -- it just keeps waiting for proper setup instead of guessing what it is.

entries.title: Calmer first boot after a full erase (firmware 1.20)
notes: New firmware 1.20: the very first boot after a full flash erase no longer prints a scary-looking storage error to the console. It was harmless -- the settings storage simply didn't exist yet on a blank chip -- but it sent a real troubleshooting session down the wrong path, so now the storage is created quietly instead.
The wizard now reminds you to close the flashing dialog before the configure step -- it can keep holding the serial port, which made a freshly-flashed, perfectly healthy display look undetectable.
No other changes.

entries.title: Complete flash sets for the browser flasher
notes: The browser flasher now writes the same complete set of parts that a developer flash writes -- including the small file that records which app slot to boot from. Previously that file was never written, which was harmless on a normal install but left booting dependent on a fallback after a full "Erase device" install.

After flashing, the wizard now tells you that native-USB displays sometimes need to be unplugged and replugged before the next step, and that the port can come back under a new name -- and points you at the flasher's log console if the device never reappears.
No firmware code changes; the firmware binaries themselves are unchanged.

entries.title: Cleaner PIN and certificate pages
notes: The Dashboard PIN page is now a tidy stack of matching cards -- Change the PIN, Lock stats viewing, Turn the PIN off (button now says Disable PIN), and a new Forgot PIN? card -- each with one uniform button that opens its own confirmation window instead of showing a pile of fields.
Changing the PIN happens in a popup with Current, New, and Repeat fields, with mistakes (mismatched PINs, wrong current PIN) explained right in the window so you can fix and retry.
Uploading a certificate also moved into a popup: two clean file-picker buttons, and Install Certificate stays disabled until both files are chosen.

Certificate rejections now say what's actually wrong in plain language -- including the classic mistakes: swapped files, a password-protected key, the wrong file entirely, or a key that doesn't match the certificate. No more raw OpenSSL error codes.
The HTTPS tip text sits in its own card with an information icon.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: The dashboard learns some moves
notes: The sidebar selection now physically glides to whatever you click instead of teleporting -- a small thing that makes the whole app feel more alive.

Buttons and controls acknowledge your cursor: filled buttons lift with a soft teal glow, choice chips warm toward their selected look, toggles show a ring before you commit, and sidebar icons give a little nudge.
All motion respects your system's reduced-motion preference -- everything falls back to instant changes.
The sidebar wears a slightly refined TinyScreen wordmark.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: Screensaver upgrades and layout polish (firmware 1.19)
notes: New firmware 1.19 (flash from the General tab after updating): the screensaver gets a brightness setting, and a new 'Temperature' style -- just the CPU temperature, big and centered, in the same green-to-red colors as the temperature page.
Screensaver brightness only ever dims: if Night Mode is set darker, the darker value wins, so an idle screen never lights up a room you've darkened.
The Screen tab is reordered to Brightness, Screensaver, Night Mode, Rotation, Aspect Ratio.
Loading placeholders on desktop now span the full width like the cards they stand in for, and the Debugging view shows placeholders while it loads instead of a blank pane.
Fixed the General tab on phones: the health badge was hanging outside the card; everything now stacks centered inside it.
Firmware versions now use a two-part scheme (1.19 instead of 1.19.0).

entries.title: Usable without a display, safer certificate actions
notes: Fixed a bug that could take down the whole General tab with a cryptic error when the display was unplugged (Safari showed "Attempted to assign to readonly property"). The unplugged experience now works as designed: your settings stay browsable from the last known state, marked with a Warning badge.

Even on a fresh install with no display ever connected, the General page now shows everything app-level -- updates, debugging, certificate, PIN, reset -- with a friendly pointer to first-time setup. Only the Layouts and Screen tabs actually need the device.
Installing a certificate and reverting to self-signed both get proper confirmation windows now, with any rejection shown right in the window so you can fix and retry.

On plain HTTP, the certificate upload area is replaced entirely by an explanation and a button that takes you to the HTTPS dashboard -- there's no longer any way to even start an upload that would send your private key over the network unencrypted.
The TinyScreen wordmark in the sidebar now spans the full width of the navigation, matching the selection pills.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: Proper confirmations, and a face for the app
notes: Reset Device now works the way you'd expect: a confirmation window (like the update one) asks if you're sure, and confirming resets the display and takes you straight to the setup wizard. No more instructions pointing at a wizard link that didn't exist.
Turning the PIN off also gets a proper confirmation window that asks for the PIN right there -- no more hunting for a field in a different card.
TinyScreen has a face now: the app icon shows up in your browser tabs and bookmarks, the sidebar wears the TinyScreen wordmark instead of plain text, and the About page leads with the app icon.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: Two dead buttons come alive
notes: The Reset button on the General tab now actually works -- it turns out it was never connected to anything, in any version. It now asks for a confirming second click, sends the reset over the serial connection, and tells you how it went.

"Turn off" in PIN settings looked dead because every message it tried to show you (including its error messages) was being written somewhere invisible. Fixed -- and pressing it with an empty Current PIN field now tells you immediately what to fill in, without counting against the wrong-PIN limit. Previously, five absent-minded clicks could lock you out of your own settings for a minute.
Opening PIN settings from a browser that isn't signed in now asks for the PIN up front, instead of letting each button inside bounce off the lock mid-click.
The dashboard and its scripts now tell browsers to re-check for new versions on every load, so after an app update you're never running a stale page against a new server.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: PIN polish and serial safety
notes: The setup wizard now works on a PIN-protected box: it shows the same PIN prompt as the dashboard when needed. Before this patch, the wizard's "flash from the ZimaBlade" path failed with unexplained errors if a PIN was set.

Two operations can no longer talk to the display at the same time (for example, two browser tabs both saving settings). Previously they could interleave on the serial connection and corrupt each other; now the second one gets a clear "try again in a few seconds" instead.

Repeated PIN lockouts now back off: each consecutive lockout doubles the wait (one minute, then two, four... up to an hour), which turns a patient guessing attack from days into months. Every lockout is also written to the app's log with the source address, so an attack is visible instead of silent.
HTTPS now explicitly refuses anything older than TLS 1.2, and every build now runs an advisory check of the app's dependencies against known vulnerabilities.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: PIN hardening patch
notes: Changing or removing the PIN now signs out every browser that was logged in under the old one. That's the whole point of changing a PIN -- if you rotated it because some device shouldn't have access anymore, that device's saved login now dies with the old PIN. (You stay signed in on the device you made the change from.)

The guess limit (five wrong tries, one-minute lockout) now covers every place a PIN can be checked, not just the login box. Before this patch, a device that was already signed in could try current-PIN guesses without limit when attempting to change the lock's settings.
The dashboard now refuses to be embedded inside other websites, closing off clickjacking tricks against a signed-in browser, and login responses are marked never-cache.
If you set your PIN on 0.9.5.0: it keeps working unchanged; the first time you change it, the new protections apply automatically.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: Optional dashboard PIN
notes: You can now protect the dashboard with a PIN (General tab -> Dashboard PIN). With it on, changing anything -- flashing, settings, updates, certificates -- asks for the PIN once per browser and remembers you for 30 days. It's off by default, and if you never turn it on, nothing changes.
An extra toggle also locks the read-only stats, for homes where CPU/disk/network numbers feel private.

The PIN is never stored in readable form (only a strongly salted hash), guessing is rate-limited (five wrong tries locks login for a minute), and changing or removing the PIN always requires the current one. Forgot it? Delete auth.json from the app's state folder and the dashboard unlocks -- someone with access to the machine itself always outranks the PIN.

The README now has a written-down Security model section: exactly what this app trusts and why, what's protected even without a PIN, and an honest explanation of the privileged-container + Docker-socket design.
No firmware changes.

entries.title: The flash path gets a test rig
notes: The three operations that take exclusive control of the display's serial connection -- flashing firmware, sending settings, and factory reset -- now have a full automated test suite. A simulated device on a real serial channel answers exactly like the board's firmware does, and a scripted stand-in for the flashing tool lets every success and failure path run without hardware. 19 new tests cover the whole dance, including making sure the stats collector always pauses before, and resumes after, every one of these operations -- even when they fail.

Those tests (and all the existing ones) now run automatically on every build, and a failing test blocks the release from publishing. Previously the test suite only ran when someone remembered to run it.
A new build check guarantees the two copies of the firmware source (the PlatformIO tree and the Arduino IDE sketch) are byte-for-byte identical, so an edit to one can never ship without the other.
No firmware, feature, or visual changes -- and no changes to the app's behavior at all; this release is entirely about catching future mistakes before they reach you.

entries.title: Infrastructure hardening
notes: The web server underneath the dashboard grew up: both the HTTP and HTTPS listeners now run on cheroot, a production-grade server, instead of Flask's built-in development server (which its own makers say not to deploy). Certificate uploads still take effect instantly, no restart needed.

The setup wizard no longer loads its flashing component from a CDN on the internet -- it's now bundled into the app itself, pinned to an exact version. The wizard now works on a ZimaBlade with no internet access, and a compromised or changed CDN copy can't affect your flasher.
Every Python library the app uses is now pinned to an exact, verified version, so two builds of the same code can't quietly ship different library behavior.

Releases now get their own Docker image tag (like :0.9.3.0) alongside :latest, so any version in this changelog is a real image you can pin or roll back to. A new build-time check also refuses to ship if the version number and changelog ever disagree.
No firmware, feature, or visual changes.

entries.title: More security tidying
notes: Certificate uploads are now accepted only over HTTPS (port 8990), so your private key is never sent across the network unencrypted. If you open the certificate manager over plain HTTP, it now tells you and offers a one-click link to the secure address instead of failing after you've picked files.

Error messages returned to the browser are now generic ('Flashing failed unexpectedly.') while the real details go to the app's logs -- so internal paths and diagnostics aren't handed out to anyone poking at the API.
Tightened the timezone validation to accept only real IANA zone names (like America/Chicago), as an extra layer of defense before that value is ever used.
No firmware, feature, or visual changes -- another safety-only patch.

entries.title: Hardening the web surface
notes: Security: the dashboard's action endpoints (flash, reset, update, certificate changes) now reject cross-site and headerless requests. Previously a random website you happened to visit could have quietly told your browser to POST commands to the dashboard on your network; that hole is closed. Normal use is unaffected -- anyone actually on your LAN, using the real dashboard, still has full access (there's still no login, by design for now).

Security: added a global 1 MiB cap on request bodies, so a malformed or malicious oversized request can't exhaust memory on a small ZimaBlade. (The certificate upload keeps its own tighter per-file limit on top.)
Under the hood: stopped parsing request bodies as JSON regardless of their declared type -- a small correctness and safety tidy-up that pairs with the cross-site protection above.
No feature or visual changes; no firmware changes. This is purely a safety patch.

entries.title: Spring cleaning
notes: No new features this round -- instead, the whole project got tidied for the road to 1.0.

The README was rewritten to describe the app as it actually is today: all seven screen layouts, the settings dashboard, drive health, the certificate manager, the real protocol fields (including the RAM-is-GiB / disks-are-decimal-GB distinction), and how to run the test suite.

Two real firmware bugs fell out of a full compiler-warnings pass: the NAS gauge's color was written as a 24-bit value that silently truncated (the on-screen lavender was an accident), and the screensaver clock's text buffer was one bad value away from truncating. Both fixed.

Duplicated code consolidated (the dashboard had four identical copies of one small helper), stale comments corrected, Python bytecode caches removed from the repo, and a .gitignore added so they stay out.

entries.title: Bring your own HTTPS certificate
notes: New in General: an HTTPS Certificate manager. Upload your own certificate and private key (Let's Encrypt or any CA, PEM format) and the browser's untrusted-certificate warning disappears -- provided you visit the dashboard by the hostname on the certificate.

Uploads are validated before they're installed (malformed files and keys that don't match the certificate are rejected with a reason), the previous pair is kept as an on-disk backup, and new connections switch to the new certificate immediately -- no restart.
Automation-friendly: a certbot deploy hook can POST renewals straight to /api/upload_cert, which matters because Let's Encrypt certificates renew every 90 days.
One-click revert generates a fresh self-signed certificate if you change your mind.

Security tightening for everyone, whether you upload or not: the private key and its folder are now locked to owner-only permissions -- previously the key was created world-readable on the host. Fixed automatically the first time the updated app starts.
If you only use plain HTTP (port 8989), nothing changes for you -- HTTPS exists mainly so the WebSerial flasher can run.

entries.title: A fuller drive card, and friendlier waiting
notes: The ZimaOS Drive card's hard drive illustration grew to span the health pill, Used, and Total lines -- no more empty corner below it.
While the dashboard fetches device info, each tab now shows softly shimmering placeholder cards shaped like the content they're standing in for, instead of a bare line of loading text.

When the real cards arrive, they ease in with a quick staggered fade -- two-tenths of a second, because the app should feel snappy, not theatrical. (If your system asks for reduced motion, everything appears instantly instead.)

entries.title: ZimaOS Drive: your storage card, health pill included
notes: New 'ZimaOS Drive' layout for the MMC and NAS pages, modeled on the ZimaOS dashboard's storage card: a little hard drive illustration, a health pill, Used and Total, and a blue usage bar along the bottom.

About that pill: the system disk's health comes from the eMMC's own JEDEC health registers (the kernel exposes them as plain files -- no special access needed). For SATA/NVMe pool drives the app now ships smartctl, but real SMART also requires the container to see the drives; without that, the pill simply doesn't appear rather than guessing.
Health goes green / amber / red for healthy, warning, and critical, checked every ten minutes so your disks aren't pestered.
When health is unknown, the card tidies itself up instead of leaving an empty spot.

entries.title: Zima App Ring, and two layouts renamed
notes: New 'Zima App Ring' layout for CPU and RAM, straight from the Zima app's widgets: a ring of 20 dots filling clockwise as usage climbs, the CPU model (politely truncated -- Intel names are novels) or RAM size up top, power draw for the CPU, and the big percentage bottom-left. Even a whisper of usage lights at least one dot.
Two layouts got more accurate names: ZimaOS Dots is now Zima App Dots, and ZimaOS Mist is now Zima App Mist. Just labels -- your saved settings are untouched.

entries.title: Graph header: steadier, and properly purple
notes: The Graph's rate arrows are now slim stroke-style arrows (stem and chevron) instead of solid triangles, matching the dashboard preview's look.
Upload purple is now the exact shade from the reference design, on both the device and the preview.

The rate readouts sit in fixed slots -- arrows, text, and baseline never shift as the numbers change width, so the header stops dancing during a busy transfer. One font size fits everything up to a two-way gigabit, verified against the actual glyph tables.

entries.title: ZimaOS Graph: watch your traffic roll by
notes: New network layout: a rolling graph of the last few minutes of traffic, download in teal and upload in purple, each a soft filled area with a bright line. Newest data on the right, one sample per second.

No axes on purpose -- the graph continuously rescales itself to the tallest thing on screen, so a quiet trickle and a full-speed download both draw an interesting picture. The live rates sit up top behind little arrows.
The rate readouts up top use compact units (Kb / Mb / Gb) and step down a font size when both numbers get long, so a two-way gigabit moment never runs off the edge.
History records in the background regardless of which layout is showing, so the moment you switch to the Graph it already has a story to tell.

entries.title: Dots fit right, RAM reads right, and the page dots retire
notes: The Dots grid no longer crowds the right edge -- its spacing is now computed from the actual panel width (a height-based scaling bug pushed it off the edge on the 240x280 screen), and the dots slimmed down a touch for comfortable margins on every panel.
The RAM dial now shows total installed memory -- the same number your OS reports (15.5 GB on a 16 GB stick, after the system's own reservations).
On the network card, 'Download' politely steps aside as 'Down..' whenever the rate text would crowd it -- measured against the actual rendered widths, not a guess.
The layouts got proper names: ZimaOS Mist, ZimaOS Dial, Zima App Bars, and ZimaOS Dots. Your saved settings carry over unchanged.
The little page-indicator dots at the bottom of the screen are gone -- swiping needs no map, and every layout gets the breathing room back.

entries.title: Network speaks Mbps, RAM shows what's free, and storage gets Dots
notes: The Bars network layout now shows rates in megabits -- the same language your speedtest uses. A 950 Mbps test previously displayed as '113.2MB' (megaBYTES per second, the identical speed in confusing clothing); now it just says 950 Mbps. The bars were already scaled to a full gigabit link and stay that way.
The RAM dial's bottom number now shows available memory instead of memory in use, as intended.

New 'Dots' layout for the MMC and NAS pages: a 12x4 grid of dots that fills column by column as the volume fills up, with the available space headlined below. The dots glow green until 75% used, amber to 95%, and red beyond -- a fuel gauge you can read from across the room.
The Dots card headlines your volume: the system disk shows as ZimaOS-HD (override with TINYSCREEN_MMC_LABEL if yours differs), and the NAS card names your attached drive.

entries.title: Bigger dial text, and a Bars layout for the network
notes: The dial's label and stats line grew to a comfortable size, and the big percentage now sits at exactly 1.5x the rows below it (two new font sizes baked into the firmware make this possible).

The network page gets an alternate 'Bars' layout: a widget-style card showing which interface the traffic is flowing through, with Upload and Download rates and slim blue activity bars that fill against a gigabit link.
The collector now reports the name of your busiest network interface (usually just eth0 on a ZimaBlade) so the card can headline it.
Pick it in the Layouts tab, same as Mist and Dial.

entries.title: Dial fit and finish
notes: The dial's ring is meatier on the device (it was rendering skinnier than intended), while the dashboard preview slimmed down a touch -- the two now carry the same visual weight.
The big percentage is now truly centered on the dial; the little % sign rides along on the right without dragging the number off-axis.

entries.title: New Dial layout for CPU and RAM
notes: CPU and RAM each get an alternate 'Dial' layout: a speedometer-style ring with its opening at the bottom, a big percentage in the middle, and the useful numbers underneath -- power draw and temperature for the CPU, gigabytes in use for RAM.
The dial's ring color follows the load: calm green up to 60%, warming to amber by 80%, and red past 95%. A box under strain announces itself from across the room.
If your hardware doesn't report CPU power draw, the dial quietly shows just the temperature instead of a bogus zero.
Pick layouts per page in the Layouts tab, same as the Mist layouts for temperature.

entries.title: The corona stretches its legs
notes: The Mist glow now reaches all the way out -- its furthest edges brush the top-left and bottom-right corners, matching the preview instead of stopping halfway. It also runs a touch brighter on the device, because the little panel swallows dark shades harder than a computer monitor does.

Particles now radiate from the corner in every direction -- straight up, straight right, and everything in between -- instead of marching single-file toward the middle. They still fade out about half a screen from home.
Fixed along the way: a faded particle sitting on the bright part of the glow could punch a dark hole in it. Particles now add their light on top of the glow instead of replacing it.

entries.title: Mist, take three -- and we have a logo
notes: The Mist glow is finally smooth. Turns out the little screen's colors are chunky enough that any ring-based gradient shows contour lines, so the glow is now drawn pixel by pixel with a touch of noise mixed in to hide the steps. No more banding.

The mist moved to the bottom-LEFT corner, where it always belonged, and the background speckle is gone entirely -- the animated layout's rising particles are the only mist particles now, and they fade out by about halfway up the screen.
The big temperature number is now roughly twice the size, in a new jumbo digits-only font. If your CPU somehow hits triple digits, it politely steps down a size instead of running off the screen.
TinyScreen has a logo! The hand-made wordmark now headlines the About tab, retiring the dashed placeholder that's been holding its seat.

entries.title: Smooth fonts and a thicker mist
notes: The screen finally types like the previews: blocky pixel text is gone, replaced by smooth proportional fonts (DejaVu Sans, rendered right on the device). Every page benefits.
The default gauges now genuinely match the dashboard previews -- title up top, rounded-cap progress arc, big clean percentage in the middle.
The Mist layout got a lot mistier: a proper glow rising from the corner, about three times the speckle, and twice the drifting particles in the animated variant.
Needs firmware v1.5.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash from the General tab.

entries.title: Layout styles arrive
notes: The layout dropdowns on the Layouts page are real now! Every page has the classic look, named 'TinyScreen (default)'.

CPU Temperature gets the first alternates: 'Mist' -- a widget-style card with a big temperature number and a colored glow rising from the corner -- and 'Mist (animated)', where little pixels drift out of the mist and fade away.
The number and the mist both shift from green through amber to red as your CPU warms up, and the dashboard previews show exactly the style you've picked.
Needs firmware v1.4.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash from the General tab.

entries.title: Sub-pages and small comforts
notes: The About page slimmed down to fit on one screen: the changelog shows just the latest release with a View more page behind it, and the credits became a tidy strip of names and faces with a Learn more page for the full write-ups.
View Logs works now -- the Debugging section opens a full page with the last firmware update's output and the app's raw status, right there when you need it.
Unplugging the screen no longer errors out the whole dashboard: everything stays browsable with a clear 'No device detected' warning.
Plus a batch of General-tab alignment fixes on desktop and mobile, and a reserved spot for the TinyScreen logo (coming soon to a repo near you).

entries.title: General tab, comfier
notes: The screen preview on the General tab is now front and center at a proper size, and device health shows as a tidy status pill -- including which ZimaBoard it's connected to.
On phones, the General tab breathes: the preview slides in under the device name, and every card's button gets its own row.

entries.title: A friendlier front page
notes: The General tab got a redesign: your device card now shows a live-style preview of the screen, uses the same naming as the setup wizard, and only offers a firmware update button when there's actually an update.
Checking for app updates is now a button, no page refresh needed -- and updating shows a proper confirmation window with the patch notes right there.

entries.title: Screen page upgrades
notes: Two new screen options: Rotation (mount your screen sideways or upside down and keep everything right-side up) and, on the 1.69-inch display, a square 1:1 aspect ratio mode. Both need firmware v1.3.0, included in this release.
Night Mode now handles daylight saving time on its own -- no more re-saving your schedule when the clocks change.
Brightness can now go all the way to off, the sliders look at home in the dark theme, and the Night Mode controls fit properly on phones.
Fixed the white flashes at the screen edges on iPhones, and locked scrolling to the page content so the toolbar stays put.

entries.title: Carousel fine-tuning
notes: Fixed the preview gauges looking slightly off-center (the colored arc wasn't sitting on its track).
When the carousel wraps to a second row on narrower windows, the thumbnails now stay the same size across rows.
'Temperature' is now 'CPU Temperature', to be clearer about what it's measuring.

entries.title: Layouts polish
notes: Fixed the save banner sticking around after saving on Safari, and made it fit comfortably on phone screens.
CPU, RAM, and Network pages are now called 'Utilization' (with tidy short names on the previews), the carousel thumbnails grew to fill the row, and the previews lost their little page-indicator dots.
On phones, each page's preview and layout picker now stack neatly in the center instead of spilling off the edge.

entries.title: Layouts, reimagined
notes: The Layouts page matches the original design vision: a visual carousel strip up top showing your pages in order (drag to rearrange, or use the little arrows), and each page in its own card below with a live-style preview and a spot for layout styles (coming soon).
Automatic Carousel is now a simple toggle, with the timing options appearing when it's on.
Saving got smarter: a banner slides down whenever you have unsaved changes and follows you between tabs, with Save and Discard right there. No more save buttons scattered around.

entries.title: Little touches
notes: The dashboard window is a bit shorter and better proportioned on desktop.
The navigation got icons -- and on phones it slims down to just the icons, so nothing scrolls sideways.

entries.title: A proper window
notes: The dashboard now lives in its own self-contained window -- sidebar and content each get their own panel, floating over the same ambient background as the setup wizard. It should feel right at home next to ZimaOS's own settings.
Works on small screens too: the window goes edge-to-edge and the navigation moves to the top.

entries.title: Wizard glow-up
notes: The setup wizard got friendlier: clearer language throughout, proper names for both screens, links to where you can buy them, and smooth transitions between steps.
Finishing setup now takes you to the dashboard (it used to drop you on an old settings page).
Retired three leftover pages from earlier in the project -- old links to them redirect to the right place.

entries.title: Reset Device
notes: The Reset button on the General tab now works: it erases the display's saved settings and walks you back through first-time setup. Handy if something gets into a weird state.
Comes with a confirm step, so a stray click can't wipe anything.
Needs firmware v1.2.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash the firmware from the General tab.

entries.title: Night Mode and Screensaver
notes: Night Mode: dim the screen (or turn it off entirely) on a daily schedule -- great for a display that lives in your bedroom or office.
Screensaver: on touch displays, show a drifting clock or blank the screen after a few idle minutes. Tap to wake.
This is the first firmware update since launch (v1.1.0) -- after updating the app, head to the General tab and hit Check for Update to flash it.

entries.title: The About page
notes: The About tab is now a real page: project info, credits for the open-source projects TinyScreen is built on, license details, and this changelog.
All of it is pulled straight from the GitHub repo, so it stays current without needing an app update.

entries.title: A tiny test balloon
notes: Added an About tab placeholder to the sidebar.
Honestly, this release mostly existed to prove the new one-click updater worked on real hardware. (It did.)

entries.title: One-click updates
notes: The Update App button now actually updates the app: one click (plus a confirm), about 30 seconds, done.
If anything goes wrong mid-update, it automatically rolls back to the version you were on and tells you what happened.
Versions now have friendly numbers like this one, instead of git commit codes.
Fixed the dashboard opening on the Layouts tab while the sidebar claimed you were on General.

entries.title: Update detection
notes: The dashboard now notices when a newer version of the app has been published and says so on the General tab.
